data.title: 10分ルール
data.text: Before buying anything non-essential, I wait ten minutes. If I still want it after ten minutes, I buy it. If I've forgotten about it, I clearly didn't need it. The rule sounds trivial, and most of the time it is—I buy the thing anyway. But roughly one in five times, the urge passes, and I realize I was buying a feeling, not a product. Over a year, those prevented purchases add up to more than a month's rent. The ten-minute rule doesn't require willpower. It requires only a pause, and a pause is free.

data.title: オフィスの犬
data.text: A company near my office allows dogs at work, and I've noticed something interesting about their employees. They take more breaks, but they seem less stressed. They walk outside more often because the dogs need it, and they talk to each other more because the dogs create natural conversation starters. The dogs don't know they're improving workplace culture. They just want treats and belly rubs. But the effect is real: a simple presence that gives people permission to pause, move, and connect. Sometimes the best workplace policy has four legs.

data.title: 早朝の時間
data.text: I used to hate mornings until I discovered what mornings are for: the things that matter but aren't urgent. Reading, stretching, writing, thinking. These tasks never survive the afternoon—they get pushed aside by meetings, emails, and other people's priorities. But at six in the morning, nobody has priorities yet. The world is quiet and your attention is whole. I don't wake up early because I'm disciplined. I wake up early because it's the only time of day that belongs entirely to me.

data.title: スキルの踊り場
data.text: Every skill has a plateau: a period where effort continues but improvement stops. The plateau feels like failure, and most people quit there, assuming they've hit their ceiling. But the plateau isn't a ceiling—it's a bridge. The brain is reorganizing what it knows, building new neural connections that will eventually produce a breakthrough. The breakthrough always arrives after the plateau, never instead of it. Knowing this doesn't make the plateau less frustrating, but it does make it survivable.
